fuzzy_prd:
We need a reusable abstraction that wraps a single persisted value — identified by a string key stored in a backing key-value store — and exposes it as an observable stream. When you write a new value it should persist to the store and push the update to every active observer. When you remove the value it should be deleted from the store and every observer should fall back to the configured default. When a new observer is attached, it should immediately receive whatever value is currently in the store (or the default if nothing is stored), without any write happening. The stream should deduplicate: if you write the same value twice in a row, the observer only fires once for the first change, not again for the repeat. Multiple observers on the same value should all receive every change in attachment order. If the value is constructed with no key at all, both write and remove should be rejected with a clear error — handle that the same way the validation layer handles empty required fields: refuse the operation and report why.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for output, let’s keep the wording exact. Observer emissions should come through as 'emit[<i>] <value>\n'. Store writes coming from put should be 'store write key=<k> value=<v>\n', and removes should be 'store remove key=<k>\n'. The operation result lines are 'put ok=<bool>\n' and 'remove ok=<bool>\n'. And for the null-key case, both operations should fail with the exact error line 'error=unsupported_operation op=<put|remove> reason=null_key\n'.

Also, 'preset' is just a side-channel write used to seed the backing store directly WITHOUT notifying existing observers, and it produces no output lines itself. The only thing that matters there is that observers attached after a preset should treat that preset value as their initial value. This is only for test setup, not normal behavior.

One other nuance on deduping: the observer only emits when the re-read value differs from the last value it emitted. So if you write the same value as the current one, it should still go through to the store, which means you still get the 'store write' line and 'put ok=true', but there is NO new observer emission. When the value is actually different, each distinct new value should emit once.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: class Preference<T>(SharedPreferences store, String? key, T defaultValue, PreferenceAdapter<T> adapter, StreamController<String> keyChanges)

trigger_keywords: what is the observer numbering scheme
what index does the first observer get
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Observers are numbered from 0 in attachment order. The first attached observer is emit[0], second is emit[1], etc. When multiple observers are active, a single put produces one store write, one 'put ok=true', then one emission per observer in attachment order (emit[0], emit[1], emit[2], etc.).

trigger_keywords: what error is produced for null key operations
what neutral error lines appear for a keyless value
what reason is reported when key is null
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When key is null, both put and remove emit: 'error=unsupported_operation op=<op> reason=null_key\n'. No store lines are produced. The error is language-neutral — reason is the fixed string 'null_key'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle that the same way the validation layer handles empty required fields: refuse the operation and report why
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Preference class throws UnsupportedError when setValue or clear is called on a null-key preference. The dispatcher catches UnsupportedError, classifies it as 'unsupported_operation', and emits 'error=unsupported_operation op=<op> reason=null_key'.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. The request has 'key', 'default', and 'ops' fields. Ops are: observe, put, remove, preset.

feature1_set_value_and_notify.json.description: Persisting a sequence of values through the value-setting operation. The store starts empty and one observer is attached, so it first receives the configured default value. Each set operation delegates the write to the backing key-value store (recording the key and the value written, in call order) and reports whether the write succeeded, and then notifies the active observer which re-reads the freshly stored value. The observer therefore sees each newly persisted value in turn.

cases.expected_output:
emit[0] default value
store write key=key value=value1
put ok=true
emit[0] value1
store write key=key value=value2
put ok=true
emit[0] value2
store write key=key value=value3
put ok=true
emit[0] value3

feature2_clear_value.json.description: Removing the stored value. The store is preset with an existing value and an observer is attached, so the observer first sees the preset value. The removal operation delegates a deletion of the key to the backing store (recorded as a remove call) and reports whether it succeeded; because the value associated with the key is now absent, the observer is notified and re-reads, seeing the configured default value.

cases.expected_output:
emit[0] stored value
store remove key=key
remove ok=true
emit[0] default value

feature3_null_key_guard.json.description: Guarding mutating operations on a value that has no key. When the value is constructed without a key, both the set operation and the removal operation are unsupported and must fail instead of touching the store. Each attempt produces a normalized error line naming the attempted operation and the reason, and no store mutation is recorded.

cases.expected_output:
error=unsupported_operation op=put reason=null_key
error=unsupported_operation op=remove reason=null_key

feature4_starts_with_latest.json.description: A freshly attached observer immediately receives the value currently held in the backing store. Between attachments the store is updated through a side channel that does NOT notify existing observers, so each newly attached observer (and only it) emits the value present at the moment it begins observing.

feature5_no_duplicate_consecutive.json.description: A single observer never receives the same value twice in a row. After the initial default emission, repeated set operations that write the same value still delegate every write to the store, but the observer only emits when the re-read value actually differs from the last value it emitted; consecutive identical writes produce no new emission, while each genuinely changed value is emitted once.

cases.expected_output:
emit[0] default value
store write key=key value=new value
put ok=true
emit[0] new value
store write key=key value=new value
put ok=true
store write key=key value=new value
put ok=true
store write key=key value=another value 1
put ok=true
emit[0] another value 1
store write key=key value=another value 2
put ok=true
emit[0] another value 2
store write key=key value=another value 3
put ok=true
emit[0] another value 3

feature6_broadcasts_to_all_listeners.jso.description: Every value change is delivered to all active observers. Three observers are attached and each first receives the default value. Each subsequent set operation delegates the write to the store once and notifies all three observers, so every observer sees the same new value, in observer-attachment order.

cases.expected_output:
emit[0] default value
emit[1] default value
emit[2] default value
store write key=key value=first change
put ok=true
emit[0] first change
emit[1] first change
emit[2] first change
store write key=key value=second change
put ok=true
emit[0] second change
emit[1] second change
emit[2] second change
